EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front and very strong mid-level disturbance moved across a highly unstable air mass across all of New York during the late afternoon and evening of July 8th. The low level jet stream was unusually strong and got entrained into the thunderstorms, thus producing scattered to widespread wind damage across Northern New York, especially St. Lawrence county. Much of the wind damage was tree and utility line damage but also some structural damage due to fallen trees. Some wind damage was estimated at 70 mph or greater.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and large branches down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front approaching from southern Ontario triggered numerous thunderstorms across central New York. These storms produced areas of 2 to 4 inches of rainfall over a short period of time. Areas of flash flooding were reported in the Southern Tier and Western Catskills regions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was one foot of water rushing across portions of County Route 4 in this area. The road was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place over the region on Thursday, July 3rd.  As a strong cold front approached from the west, a broken line of thunderstorms developed over central New York and moved eastward during the afternoon hours.  Aided by powerful winds aloft, the storms moved from west to east over the region between the late afternoon and early evening hours.  Many of the storms produced strong winds, causing damage to trees and power lines over much of the region.  The most concentrated damage occurred from one particularly severe thunderstorm, which caused intense damage between West Winfield in Herkimer County towards Minden in Montgomery County.  A National Weather Service Storm Survey determined a series of microbursts produced wind damage up to 100 MPH in these areas.  A few of the storms also produced large hail as well.||As the cold front moved through the area by later in the evening, the storms moved east of the region and the threat for severe weather ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed on the south side of Rhinebeck due to thunderstorm winds.  Many power lines were downed as a result of the fallen trees.  In addition, at least one home was damaged due to a fallen tree on King Road.  Several roads needed to be closed due to downed trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mahwah River at Suffern exceeded its flood stage of 4.0 feet at 5:45 pm EST, crested at 5.0 ft. at 11:15 pm and fell back within its banks at 7:45 pm on May 1.  Storm total rainfall amounts reported across the county ranged from 2.85 inches in Suffern to 3.78 inches in Spring Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was stalled from southwest Ontario across central Lake Erie into northwest Pennsylvania. The front remained nearly stationary through the night and morning of the 21st and was the focus of steady, often moderate to heavy, rain over Chautauqua County. Measured rainfall reports included: 3.71 inches at Mayville, 3.26 inches as Bemus Point, 3.25 inches at Falconer, and 3.07 inches at the Jamestown airport. The heavy rains produced flash flooding mainly across the southern part of the county. Roads closed included: Route 62 in Ellington, Route 394 in Mayville, Lakewood and Jamestown; Jones Avenue, Gifford Avenue and Kiantone Road in Jamestown; Fairmont Avenue in Lakewood; and Allen Street and Allen Street in Falconer. In Falconer, a culvert washed out on County Route 380 near Interstate 86. The heavy rains also caused French Creek to overflow its banks in the Town of French Creek. In Falconer, the Chadakoin River crested at 4.4 feet. Flood stage there is 4 feet. A State of Emergency was declared in the county and the resulting damages were enough to warrant the county inclusion in a State Disaster Declaration.
Colder air moving in on the back side of low pressure departing off the East Coast changed rain to locally heavy snow during the morning of the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy snow snarled traffic during the morning rush. The NWS office in Upton and a trained spotter in Baiting Hollow reported snowfall of 2.5 and 2.7 inches, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place over the region on Tuesday, July 8th.   The region was ahead of a strong cold front that was moving eastward from the Great Lakes Region.  Aided by a strong jet streak aloft and a potent upper level shortwave trough, the cold front triggered a line of thunderstorms that produced damaging winds across western and central New York.||The line of thunderstorms reached eastern New York during the evening hours.  It continued to produce strong winds, which caused damage across the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley and eventually into the Saratoga area, Capital Region and Hudson Valley as well. Many trees and power lines were downed across Herkimer County.  In addition, a weak, brief tornado also occurred in the southeastern Adirondacks in North Creek.   The line of thunderstorms moved eastward and weakened as they reached the border with New England by the late evening hours.  As a result, the threat for severe weather ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down and on wires due to thunderstorm winds in Ohio.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and the public reported widespread snowfall of 15 to 16 inches, plus freezing rain accretion of 2 tenths of an inch in Peekskill.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey determined that a brief EF0 tornado occurred in the village of North Creek.  The tornado was accompained by a microburst, which occurred to the south of the tornado track.  The tornado tracked for about one mile, beginning near Route 28 in North Creek and moved north-northeast.  The tornado lifted after crossing the Hudson River on Bird Pond Road.  Most of the damage occurred to hardwood and softwood trees along the path, with the only damage to structures due to fallen trees.  Video evidence of the tornado was provided by a local resident through emergency services.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place over the region on Tuesday, July 8th.   The region was ahead of a strong cold front that was moving eastward from the Great Lakes Region.  Aided by a strong jet streak aloft and a potent upper level shortwave trough, the cold front triggered a line of thunderstorms that produced damaging winds across western and central New York.||The line of thunderstorms reached eastern New York during the evening hours.  It continued to produce strong winds, which caused damage across the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley and eventually into the Saratoga area, Capital Region and Hudson Valley as well. Many trees and power lines were downed across Herkimer County.  In addition, a weak, brief tornado also occurred in the southeastern Adirondacks in North Creek.   The line of thunderstorms moved eastward and weakened as they reached the border with New England by the late evening hours.  As a result, the threat for severe weather ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down in Edinburg due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of thunderstorms repeatedly moved along a warm front in the vicinity of the Finger Lakes region. Radar rainfall estimates indicated a narrow band of 4 to 5 inches of rain occurred over the central portion of Yates and Seneca counties. This rainfall resulted in devastating flash flooding  in Penn Yan, NY where roads and buildings were destroyed by flood waters. Total public damages are estimated between 10 and 12 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Catastrophic flash flooding was occurring in the downtown area of the Village of Penn Yan. Jacobs Brook became a raging torrent of water as it traveled into, and under the center of the village. The hardest hit areas were in the vicinity of Elm Street and Champlin Avenue where roads buckled, parking lots caved in, and the Owl's Nest Community Center collapsed. Tractor-trailer container boxes were seen floating down the streets, where they collided into the Wagner restaurant causing significant structural damage. Foundations of several homes were washed away during the flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed along the northern Gulf Coast on February 12th, and moved to near Cape Hatteras and the Virginia capes by the morning of the 13th. The intensifying low then headed northeast, passing east of Long Island and into the Gulf of Maine early the morning of the 14th, bringing heavy snow and ice to Southeast New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Central Park reported snowfall of 12.5 inches. Freezing rain accretion up to 3 tenths of an inch also occurred per observations from surrounding areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A harsh winter built a much above normal snow pack in the Black River basin and this snow pack contributed significantly to flooding in that region. At the beginning of the month, snow water equivalent averaged about twice the normal value on the Tug Hill.  Temperatures averaged much above normal the second week in April  with Watertown reaching 79 degrees and setting a record high on the 13th  and then reaching 80 the next day.  This was immediately followed by a modest rainfall with between three-quarters of an inch and an inch falling in the basin.  The combination of warm temperatures and rain melted up to 8 inches of snow water equivalent and resulted in widespread flooding in the Eastern Lake Ontario region.  The Black River reached moderate flood stage cresting at 13.81 feet at 05:15 EST on the 17th which is the 3rd highest on record. The Watertown gauge remained in flood stage for about 5 days. The Black river reached minor flood stage at Boonville cresting at 10.61 feet at 01:30 EST on the 16th, and the tributary Moose River reached moderate flood stage cresting at 13.45 feet at 16:45 EST on the 15th.  In addition to forecast points, the Beaver River, West Branch of the Oswegatchie River, and the Salmon River also flooded.  In all, this resulted in numerous road closures, damage to farmland and some residential structures. Several dozen homes were evacuated in Jefferson and Lewis counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place over the region on Thursday, July 3rd.  As a strong cold front approached from the west, a broken line of thunderstorms developed over central New York and moved eastward during the afternoon hours.  Aided by powerful winds aloft, the storms moved from west to east over the region between the late afternoon and early evening hours.  Many of the storms produced strong winds, causing damage to trees and power lines over much of the region.  The most concentrated damage occurred from one particularly severe thunderstorm, which caused intense damage between West Winfield in Herkimer County towards Minden in Montgomery County.  A National Weather Service Storm Survey determined a series of microbursts produced wind damage up to 100 MPH in these areas.  A few of the storms also produced large hail as well.||As the cold front moved through the area by later in the evening, the storms moved east of the region and the threat for severe weather ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed due to thunderstorm winds in Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain preceded the passage of a cold front. In southern Allegany county, up to three inches of rain fell in a short amount of time. A flash flood resulted on a tributary of the Genesee River in Shongo, located in the Town of Willing. About a dozen homes were quickly inundated with water along State Route 19. The local fire department evacuated some residents from their homes and pumped basements.

A surface cold front passed through the region during the afternoon on Thursday, August 7th.  This front was associated with a strong upper level trough, with rather cold temperatures aloft.   As a result, some scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours and several of the storms produced hail. A few of the storms produced severe hail, especially across the Glens Falls-Saratoga Region.  One of the storms also produced damaging wind gusts as well. The threat for storms ended by the late afternoon, as the cold front pushed south and the upper level trough moved away from the area to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported during a thunderstorm in Schuylerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system associated with a large cutoff low pressure system over the Midwest and Lower Great Lakes region caused periods of heavy rain, which resulted in continued flooding across Westchester and Rockland Counties as well as the Bronx in New York City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mahwah River at Suffern remained in flood from April 30. Water levels were falling at midnight EST and the river fell back within its banks at 7:45 pm EST on May 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system associated with a large cutoff low pressure system over the Midwest and Lower Great Lakes region caused periods of heavy rain, which resulted in continued flooding across Westchester and Rockland Counties as well as the Bronx in New York City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bronx River at New York Botanical Gardens remained in flood from April 30. The river crested at 3.68 ft. at 3:00 am EST on May 1 and fell back within its banks at 10:15 am EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low pressure area moved from the Great Lakes towards the Northeast between Wednesday, August 20th and Friday August 22nd.  Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms occurred across the entire region during this time period.  ||During the evening on Wednesday, August 20th, slow moving thunderstorms produced two to four inches of rain across parts of the Mohawk Valley and Sacandaga Region.  Despite this rainfall, no flooding was reported, but this rainfall led to ponding of water on roadways and helped to moisten up the ground conditions.  ||Another batch of heavy rainfall due to slow moving thunderstorms occurred the next day on Thursday, August 21st during the evening hours.  Once again, several inches of rainfall occurred across the central Mohawk Valley.  With the ground saturated due to the recent rainfall, small streams and creek quickly overspread their banks.  This led to flash flooding across parts of west-central Montgomery and northern Schoharie Counties, with many roads washed out and closed.  At least 15 roads were closed in Montgomery County, including an onramp for the New York State Thruway, and a State of Emergency was issued for the county due to the flooding.  The flooding caused sewage treatments plans to be inundated with water and a boil water advisory was issued for several days due to the local water supply being compromised.    ||Some lighter rain showers occurred on Friday, August 22nd.   Most of the damage was already done, although another minor mudslide occurred due to the already weakened ground conditions.  Once showers ended by the afternoon of Friday, August 22nd, the water was able to recede and the ground conditions started to finally dry out.  All road were reopened by Monday, August 25th at 9 am and the State of Emergency was lifted on the 25th as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous basements were inundated with water due to flash flooding from thunderstorm heavy rainfall in Sharon Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system associated with a large cutoff low pressure system over the Midwest and Lower Great Lakes region caused periods of heavy rain, which resulted in continued flooding across Westchester and Rockland Counties as well as the Bronx in New York City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northbound Hutchinson River Pkwy. was closed between exit 7 and exit 12.  Additionally, the Saw Mill River Pkwy. was closed southbound from exit 16 to Farragut Pkwy. and northbound between exits 20 and 21 in Elmsford due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The westbound Long Island Expressway was closed near Willis Ave. in Roslyn Heights due to flooding.  Additionally, the basement of a house in Hillside Manor at the intersection of Brussel Dr. and Herbert Dr. was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place over the region on Thursday, July 3rd.  As a strong cold front approached from the west, a broken line of thunderstorms developed over central New York and moved eastward during the afternoon hours.  Aided by powerful winds aloft, the storms moved from west to east over the region between the late afternoon and early evening hours.  Many of the storms produced strong winds, causing damage to trees and power lines over much of the region.  The most concentrated damage occurred from one particularly severe thunderstorm, which caused intense damage between West Winfield in Herkimer County towards Minden in Montgomery County.  A National Weather Service Storm Survey determined a series of microbursts produced wind damage up to 100 MPH in these areas.  A few of the storms also produced large hail as well.||As the cold front moved through the area by later in the evening, the storms moved east of the region and the threat for severe weather ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed and fell on a building due to thunderstorm winds 2 miles southeast of Greenfield Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a pre-frontal trough and a passing short wave triggered an isolated severe thunderstorm over eastern Orange County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was struck by lightning on Union Avenue in Newburgh.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a pre-frontal trough and a passing short wave triggered an isolated severe thunderstorm over eastern Orange County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in South Blooming Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low pressure area moved from the Great Lakes towards the Northeast between Wednesday, August 20th and Friday August 22nd.  Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms occurred across the entire region during this time period.  ||During the evening on Wednesday, August 20th, slow moving thunderstorms produced two to four inches of rain across parts of the Mohawk Valley and Sacandaga Region.  Despite this rainfall, no flooding was reported, but this rainfall led to ponding of water on roadways and helped to moisten up the ground conditions.  ||Another batch of heavy rainfall due to slow moving thunderstorms occurred the next day on Thursday, August 21st during the evening hours.  Once again, several inches of rainfall occurred across the central Mohawk Valley.  With the ground saturated due to the recent rainfall, small streams and creek quickly overspread their banks.  This led to flash flooding across parts of west-central Montgomery and northern Schoharie Counties, with many roads washed out and closed.  At least 15 roads were closed in Montgomery County, including an onramp for the New York State Thruway, and a State of Emergency was issued for the county due to the flooding.  The flooding caused sewage treatments plans to be inundated with water and a boil water advisory was issued for several days due to the local water supply being compromised.    ||Some lighter rain showers occurred on Friday, August 22nd.   Most of the damage was already done, although another minor mudslide occurred due to the already weakened ground conditions.  Once showers ended by the afternoon of Friday, August 22nd, the water was able to recede and the ground conditions started to finally dry out.  All road were reopened by Monday, August 25th at 9 am and the State of Emergency was lifted on the 25th as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 67 was closed between Fort Johnson and Johnstown due to flooding and a mudslide from thunderstorm heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 3 thunderstorms broke out in warm unstable air ahead of a slow moving cold front. Some storms trained over southeast Franklin County and southwest Clinton County New York, producing two to 3 inches of rain in less than 3 hours. The return interval for one hour duration rainfall was near a one hundred year event, and isolated flash flooding resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A culvert was washed out on Fletcher Farm Road, closing the road down to one lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front passed through the region on Wednesday, August 6th.  This front was aided by an upper level shortwave through that moved from Pennsylvania towards southern New York.  As a result, a few isolated thunderstorms developed across far southern areas during the late afternoon hours.  One of these storms became severe, producing damaging wind gusts and large hail as it passed across eastern Ulster and southwestern Dutchess counties.  The threat for storms ended by the evening, as the cold front pushed south across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down near New Paltz as a result of thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite being behind a cold front, the region remained in a warm and humid air mass.  This allowed the atmosphere to become quite unstable.   ||As yet another cold front approached the region during the afternoon hours, clusters of thunderstorms developed across eastern upstate New York. For the third day in a row, thunderstorms produced reports of wind damage across the region, with a few reports of large hail as well, as the storms moved from west to east.  ||The threat for storms ended during the evening hours, as the cold front moved across the area and into New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported during a thunderstorm in the town of New Scotland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place on the evening of Tuesday, June 17th.   A strong cold front moved across the region from west to east between late in the evening on Tuesday, June 17th and the early morning hours of Wednesday, June 18th.  Along and just ahead of this boundary, a line of severe thunderstorms in the form of a squall line raced from west to east across the area.  These storms produced significant wind damage across eastern New York.  The thunderstorms weakened somewhat as they reached the border with western New England as instability started to wane.  The threat for thunderstorms ended with the passage of the cold front by the late night hours, as drier and less humid air worked into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down in Woodstock due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and humid air mass was in place across the region on Wednesday, June 25th.  As a slow moving cold front approached from the west, bands of heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the boundary.  As these showers and thunderstorms repeatedly passed over the same locations, the heavy rainfall led to significant runoff, which caused flash flooding in some areas.  Many roads were closed due to the flooding and some homes were affected by water as well. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving into the Ohio Valley on January 2 redeveloped and intensified along the Mid Atlantic coast, bringing heavy snow to southeast New York before moving out to sea on the third of January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and humid air mass was in place across the region on Wednesday, June 25th.  As a slow moving cold front approached from the west, bands of heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the boundary.  As these showers and thunderstorms repeatedly passed over the same locations, the heavy rainfall led to significant runoff, which caused flash flooding in some areas.  Many roads were closed due to the flooding and some homes were affected by water as well.  In addition, a few of the storms produced wind damage to trees and some damaging lightning strikes.  ||The threat for heavy rainfall and flooding ended late on the evening of June 25th, as the storm's cold front passed through the region from west to east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms led to flash flooding 1 mile east-northeast of Red Rock.  Flooding on County Route 24 caused a portion of the road to wash out and the route was impassable.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several creeks in the area came out of their banks and flooded numerous roads in the Jeffersonville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary and a strong upper level disturbance helped develop numerous severe and torrential rain producing thunderstorms. Thunderstorms which repeatedly moved over the same locations along the Southern Tier of New York produced rainfall estimates of 3 to 4 inches in a short amount of time. This resulted in widespread areas of minor urban flooding and isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along Old Route 17 and Route 41 near Deposit and Windsor. Several creeks were out of their banks in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong low pressure system, for late July, descended from the Upper Great Lakes region across central New York. During the evening between July 27th and 28th, thunderstorm clusters formed in an excessively moist airmass ahead of a warm frontal boundary. These storms resulted in periods of torrential rainfall with local totals in excess of 3 inches in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streams were out of their banks. Flood waters were flowing through a residence along State Route 415 near Cohocton.

During the evening between July 27th and 28th, thunderstorm clusters formed in an excessively moist airmass ahead of a warm frontal boundary. These storms resulted in periods of torrential rainfall with local totals in excess of 3 inches in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported near the intersection of State Route 415 and State Route 121, closing both roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred across several State highways with considerable damage to the road shoulders and culverts. Numerous basements were pumped out in the Village of Rushville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary and a strong upper level disturbance helped develop numerous severe thunderstorms. Favorable and strong wind shear in the lower and middle layers of the atmosphere resulted in damaging winds and tornadoes across central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado with estimated maximum wind speeds of 90 MPH had a path width of 270 yards and a path length of 3/4 of a mile. Cosmetic damage occurred to siding, roofing and chimneys to two residences. Numerous yard ornaments and rail fencing was displaces to the left hand side of the path. Catastrophic tree damage with blow downs, snap-offs and twisting of hardwoods and large hemlock trees occurred in the core of the path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system moving across the region led to the development of numerous severe thunderstorms across central New York during the afternoon and evening hours of July 8th. With a tremendous favorable atmospheric wind pattern present, severe thunderstorms resulted in widespread wind damage along with several  tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado tracked for approximately 1.3 miles through a residential area near Utica NY. The tornado was rated as an EF1 with maximum estimated wind speeds of 90 MPH. Dozens of trees were chopped up or felled by the tornado and one residence lost two walls of an attached garage. Several other residences experienced shingle and siding damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across the region in a warm, humid air mass during the afternoon hours as a cold front approached from the west. Several of the thunderstorms produced strong, damaging winds. Damage was mainly reported as downed trees and wires however there were limited reports of structural damage in Fulton. In Phelps, a large tree was downed by the strong winds. The main base of the tree was reported to be eight to nine feet in diameter. One of the two main branches of the tree, about four feet in diameter and originally about fifteen feet off the ground, fell onto a car driving by. The tree fell directly across the front seat of the vehicle. Both persons in the car were crushed. Pronounced dead at the scene were the driver, a 34-year-old male, and his 28-year-old  wife.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees and wires downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of rainfall and warm temperatures resulted in ice jams on several Buffalo area creeks and streams. Minor flooding was reported with only limited damage reported. There were several roads that were inundated and closed. Among those reported:  Rix, Dana and Oliver Streets in Silver Creek as Silver Creek jammed; Route 60 in Cassadaga where Canadaway Creek jammed, Back Creek Road and Mill Street in the Town of Boston where Eighteen Mile Creek jammed; and Route 240 in Colden where the West Branch of Cazenovia Creek jammed. Portions of Cazenovia Park were also inundated. Several basements were also flooded.

A persistent upper level low pressure system across northern New York and southern Canada combined with moist and unstable air to produce periods of slow moving showers and thunderstorms with torrential downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were closed due to water rushing across them near the Lourdes camp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front sweeping across the lower Great Lakes was preceded and accompanied by severe thunderstorms. The thunderstorm winds produced widespread damage throughout the region. Trees and power lines were downed. The highest recorded gust was 66 mph at Fort Drum. Some specific reports of damage included:  trees down on Route 5 in Portland, Rockspring Road in Ashord, Old Chautauqua Road in Gerry, Allen Street in South Dayton, Boston-Colden Road in Colden, Route 20 in Pavilion, and Locust Street in Honeoye. Some of the trees were measured to 30 inches in diameter. The falling trees and limbs damaged several homes and automobiles in Marion, Scriba, West Rush and Martinsburg. In Farmington, a stretch of ten power poles was blown over by the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported trees and wires down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front and very strong mid-level disturbance moved across a highly unstable air mass across all of New York during the late afternoon and evening of July 8th. The low level jet stream was unusually strong and got entrained into the thunderstorms, thus producing scattered to widespread wind damage across Northern New York, especially St. Lawrence county. Much of the wind damage was tree and utility line damage but also some structural damage due to fallen trees. Some wind damage was estimated at 70 mph or greater.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel cloud caught on videotape and reviewed by NWS Burlington office...no evidence of touchdown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system associated with a large cutoff low pressure system over the Midwest and Lower Great Lakes region caused periods of heavy rain, which resulted in flooding across portions of New York City, Nassau, Rockland and Westchester Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain coinciding with high tide on the Atlantic Ocean caused 79th St. in Howard Beach to become flooded and impassable.  Two feet of water was estimated to be covering the road.  A car was also submerged in flood waters on 78th St. between 155th and 156th Avenues.  Tides were astronomically high due to a new moon on April 29.  Storm total rainfall amounts reported across the county ranged from 4.96 inches at John F. Kennedy International Airport to 5.98 inches in Middle Village.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms dropped between two- and two-and-a-half-inches of rain across parts of the Genesee valley and western southern tier. The torrential rains produced flash flooding with roads inundated with flowing water in parts of Darien, New Albion, Bennington, Conewango and Leon. In Bennington several residents had to be evacuated by the local Fire Department.

A surface cold front passed through the region during the afternoon on Thursday, August 7th.  This front was associated with a strong upper level trough, with rather cold temperatures aloft.   As a result, some scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours and several of the storms produced hail. A few of the storms produced severe hail, especially across the Glens Falls-Saratoga Region.  One of the storms also produced damaging wind gusts as well. The threat for storms ended by the late afternoon, as the cold front pushed south and the upper level trough moved away from the area to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported during a thunderstorm at the intersection of State Route 43 and Gettle Road in Averill Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front passed through the region on Wednesday, August 6th.  This front was aided by an upper level shortwave through that moved from Pennsylvania towards southern New York.  As a result, a few isolated thunderstorms developed across far southern areas during the late afternoon hours.  One of these storms became severe, producing damaging wind gusts and large hail as it passed across eastern Ulster and southwestern Dutchess counties.  The threat for storms ended by the evening, as the cold front pushed south across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported during a thunderstorm on Macghee Road in Red Oaks Mill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of thunderstorms repeatedly moved along a warm front in the vicinity of the Finger Lakes region. Radar rainfall estimates indicated a narrow band of 4 to 5 inches of rain occurred over the central portion of Yates and Seneca counties. This rainfall resulted in devastating flash flooding  in Penn Yan, NY where roads and buildings were destroyed by flood waters. Total public damages are estimated between 10 and 12 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A road was washed out on County Route 131.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in a hot, humid airmass. An isolated storm in Lewis County downed trees on Cherry Street in Lyons Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moving into a moisture rich environment over south central New York triggered several rounds of heavy rain producing thunderstorms across the region. Localized rainfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches produced areas of flash flooding in portions of the Central Southern Tier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several road washouts were reported along County Routes 76 and 54A.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed in a warm, humid air mass during the afternoon hours. These were followed by a large area of showers and thunderstorms associated with low pressure moving across the Great Lakes into southern Ontario and then Quebec.  Several of the thunderstorms produced strong, damaging winds. Damage was mainly reported as downed trees and wires however there were some reports of structural and other damage. These included, for example, the soffit blown off a house in Marion, several automobiles damaged by falling trees, and ten utility poles downed in Spencerport.  The thunderstorms also produced hail up to one-and-one-quarter inch. Hail was reported in Castile, near Windecker and in Lowville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed power poles and wires.

A cluster of thunderstorms crossed the southern tier during the afternoon and produced damaging winds across parts of Cattaraugus County. The winds downed trees and power lines in the City of Olean. Damage was reported on Main Street, West Riverside Drive, Garden Avenue and South Barry Street.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees and wires down on a house on South Barry Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt from a late season snowpack combined with heavy rain produced widespread flooding across northern New York. Four to six inches of water was released from the snowpack over April 10 to 15 when daytime highs reached the 60s and 70s, and overnight lows remained well above freezing. Rivers were brought to near bankfull or minor flood levels from snowmelt alone. Rain developed along and ahead of a cold front on April 15, and forced rivers out of their banks. Freezing temperatures returned by the morning of April 16, which slowed or halted the runoff, and flooding gradually subsided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall and snowmelt combined to produce flooding in northern Franklin County. The Salmon River and its tributaries caused the most flood trouble, with numerous road closures, washouts, and evacuations around Malone and Fort Covington. In Malone, College Avenue and Willow, Lafayette, and Patnode Streets were flooded and some residences were evacuated. At least 4 culverts were washed out. Downstream in Fort Covington, Drum Street was closed due to flooding, and additional roads were flooded by the Little Salmon River in Bombay. In Hogansburg, the St. Regis River flooded village roads including Church, McNeil, St. Regis, River, Gray, Mill, and Pearl Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt from a late season snowpack combined with heavy rain produced widespread flooding across northern New York. Four to six inches of water was released from the snowpack over April 10 to 15 when daytime highs reached the 60s and 70s, and overnight lows remained well above freezing. Rivers were brought to near bankfull or minor flood levels from snowmelt alone. Rain developed along and ahead of a cold front on April 15, and forced rivers out of their banks. Freezing temperatures returned by the morning of April 16, which slowed or halted the runoff, and flooding gradually subsided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall and snowmelt combined to produce flooding across St. Lawrence County. The Grass and St. Regis Rivers were particularly hard hit, and the Oswegatchie and its tributaries continued to run high from flooding in the previous week. Along the St. Regis, two homes were evacuated in Brasher Center, and areas in Winthrop and Brasher Falls were flooded. In Brasher Center, the Main Street Bridge and County Rte 53 were closed. Throughout Stockholm other bridges over the St. Regis were closed due to flooding, and State Rte 420 Extension and Barnage Road were closed. On the Grass River, several cottages were surrounded by water in Madrid along County Route 36. Elsewhere across the county there were numerous roads closed, including Route 184 at Pope Mills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low pressure area moved from the Great Lakes towards the Northeast between Wednesday, August 20th and Friday August 22nd.  Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms occurred across the entire region during this time period.  ||During the evening on Wednesday, August 20th, slow moving thunderstorms produced two to four inches of rain across parts of the Mohawk Valley and Sacandaga Region.  Despite this rainfall, no flooding was reported, but this rainfall led to ponding of water on roadways and helped to moisten up the ground conditions.  ||Another batch of heavy rainfall due to slow moving thunderstorms occurred the next day on Thursday, August 21st during the evening hours.  Once again, several inches of rainfall occurred across the central Mohawk Valley.  With the ground saturated due to the recent rainfall, small streams and creek quickly overspread their banks.  This led to flash flooding across parts of west-central Montgomery and northern Schoharie Counties, with many roads washed out and closed.  At least 15 roads were closed in Montgomery County, including an onramp for the New York State Thruway, and a State of Emergency was issued for the county due to the flooding.  The flooding caused sewage treatments plans to be inundated with water and a boil water advisory was issued for several days due to the local water supply being compromised.    ||Some lighter rain showers occurred on Friday, August 22nd.   Most of the damage was already done, although another minor mudslide occurred due to the already weakened ground conditions.  Once showers ended by the afternoon of Friday, August 22nd, the water was able to recede and the ground conditions started to finally dry out.  All road were reopened by Monday, August 25th at 9 am and the State of Emergency was lifted on the 25th as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide occurred near the intersection of Happy Hollow Road and Old Fort Plain Road due to recent heavy rainfall from slow moving thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across the area interacted with a very moist airmass which produced showers and thunderstorms with damaging winds and torrential downpours resulting in flash flooding portions of southeast New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hutchinson River Pkwy. was closed between exits 10 and 12 in Mt. Vernon due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system moving across the region led to the development of numerous severe thunderstorms across central New York during the afternoon and evening hours of July 8th. With a tremendous favorable atmospheric wind pattern present, severe thunderstorms resulted in widespread wind damage along with several  tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down with estimated maximum wind speeds of 80 to 90 MPH. It traveled with a path length of 1 mile and a maximum path width of 600 yards. This tornado damaged a pavilion, lightly damaged a home and uprooted numerous trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary resulted in moist and unstable air present over central New York. An approaching upper level system provided the forcing needed to generate severe, and torrential rain producing thunderstorms across central New York. The excessively moist airmass resulted in areas of significant flash flooding over parts of the Western Catskills region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded in the Youngsville area. Eyewitness reports and photographs indicated water rushing across yards and streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary resulted in moist and unstable air present over central New York. An approaching upper level system provided the forcing needed to generate severe, and torrential rain producing thunderstorms across central New York. The excessively moist airmass resulted in areas of significant flash flooding over parts of the Western Catskills region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency services reported several roads flooded in the Hortonville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary resulted in moist and unstable air present over central New York. An approaching upper level system provided the forcing needed to generate severe, and torrential rain producing thunderstorms across central New York. The excessively moist airmass resulted in areas of significant flash flooding over parts of the Western Catskills region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe flooding was occurring along many county, State and local roads in the area. A bridge over County Route 52 was over topped by flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system moving across the region led to the development of numerous severe thunderstorms across central New York during the afternoon and evening hours of July 8th. With a tremendous favorable atmospheric wind pattern present, severe thunderstorms resulted in widespread wind damage along with several  tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downburst winds impacted a 10 mile long area by about one mile wide. Most of the damage was sporadic until near Route 365 and Fairchild Road. Just south of the Stop and Go on Route 365, hundreds of hardwood trees were uprooted and snapped. Some minor damage to a house was found. Strongest winds were found here and estimated to be between 85 and 95 MPH. The damage path continued northeast across Route 365. Two more separate blow downs were found near Twin Rock Road. Winds here were between 75 and 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary and a strong upper level disturbance helped develop numerous severe thunderstorms. Favorable and strong wind shear in the lower and middle layers of the atmosphere resulted in damaging winds and tornadoes across central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado producing winds speeds up to 105 MPH and a path length of 3.5 miles and a path width of 125 yards touched down near Altay Road at 434 PM EDT in the Town of Reading and tracked east snapping and uprooting many trees along it's path. The tornado blew a canoe 150 yards from a pond and wrapped it around a pole. There were numerous large splinters from trees and branches impaled in the ground. The tornado partially lifted a roof off of a house as it tracked into the woods. The home was severely damaged. The tornado continued east causing only tree damage before it lifted by Route 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the Great Lakes and interacted with a warm and humid air mass over central New York being drawn northward from Hurricane Arthur. Favorable strong wind parameters resulted in numerous high wind reports along with an occasional large hail report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of trees were blown down with some damage to structures being reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak warm front that moved in from the Great Lakes region allowed for severe thunderstorms to develop across portions of central New York. Strong and organized low level wind shear resulted in damaging winds from all severe storms during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many trees were blown down including on top of cars, power lines and a storage shed. Trees were also blown down in the Town of Lisle. Damage reports ranged from 401 pm to 417 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system moving across the region led to the development of numerous severe thunderstorms across central New York during the afternoon and evening hours of July 8th. With a tremendous favorable atmospheric wind pattern present, severe thunderstorms resulted in widespread wind damage along with several  tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A confirmed 60-70 MPH microburst went through the city of Rome. It caused some branches to blow down and uproot a few trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the Great Lakes and interacted with a warm and humid air mass over central New York being drawn northward from Hurricane Arthur. Favorable strong wind parameters resulted in numerous high wind reports along with an occasional large hail report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downburst wind damage occurred from a thunderstorm that extended from near Earlville NY to Poolville NY. The majority of the damage was to trees with most trees being uprooted.  There were a few areas where trees were snapped.  The core of the damage where winds were estimated at 90 MPH was to a farm where a barn was blown off of its foundation by a few inches. A tin roof was partially peeled off and several large hardwood trees were uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass led to the development of several severe thunderstorms across central New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central New York was between two cold fronts. Marginal instability with weak lift resulted in a couple of severe thunderstorms during the afternoon hours.

A warm and humid air mass was in place over the region on Thursday, July 3rd.  As a strong cold front approached from the west, a broken line of thunderstorms developed over central New York and moved eastward during the afternoon hours.  Aided by powerful winds aloft, the storms moved from west to east over the region between the late afternoon and early evening hours.  Many of the storms produced strong winds, causing damage to trees and power lines over much of the region.  The most concentrated damage occurred from one particularly severe thunderstorm, which caused intense damage between West Winfield in Herkimer County towards Minden in Montgomery County.  A National Weather Service Storm Survey determined a series of microbursts produced wind damage up to 100 MPH in these areas.  A few of the storms also produced large hail as well.||As the cold front moved through the area by later in the evening, the storms moved east of the region and the threat for severe weather ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike was reported to have caused a fire to a home at 14 Temple Street in Gloversville.  The home's roof sustained heavy damage as a result of the fire. Most of the top section of the house was also destroyed, although firefighters were able to prevent it from spreading to other houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place over upstate New York during the afternoon and evening hours on Monday, July 7th, which allowed the atmosphere to become quite unstable.  As an upper level shortwave trough moved towards the area, clusters of thunderstorms developed.  These thunderstorms, although not overly tall, were able to bring very strong winds aloft down to the surface, producing damaging wind gusts to trees and power lines.  Some of the storms produced small hail as well.  The threat for severe thunderstorms ended by the late evening hours, as the shortwave trough moved east of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down near Barnerville due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sudden gust of wind blew an inflatable bounce house measuring 10 feet by 10 feet in size into the air during the afternoon on May 12th, 2014 at 22 Ferry Blvd in South Glens Falls, New York. Three children inside the bounce house fell out of the house as it blew through the air, including two young boys, who fell from a height of at least 15 feet. The two boys were seriously injured as a result of the incident and were thrown a large distance from the where the accident occurred.  After the children fell out of the bounce house, it blew more than 50 feet through the air and landed in a field of a nearby middle school (Oliver W. Winch Middle School). One boy was hospitalized until late May and the other until mid June with significant injuries.  The maximum wind gust measured that afternoon 2.7 miles away at Glens Falls Airport was 30 MPH.  The weather balloon upper air sounding at Albany that morning suggested winds could gust as high as 40 MPH.

Ahead of a slow moving cold front, a southerly flow transported plenty of moisture into the region during the day on Tuesday, June 3rd.  With a moist and unstable air mass in place, plenty of slow moving showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the approaching boundary.  Some of these thunderstorms produced very heavy rain in a short period of time.  This led to some flash flooding, especially in urban and poor drainage areas.  In addition, a few of the thunderstorms also produced strong wind gusts, which caused damage to trees and power lines.  By the late evening, the threat for thunderstorms ended, as the frontal boundary pushed through and less humid air moved into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down on Woods Road in Clermont due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place over the region on Tuesday, July 8th.   The region was ahead of a strong cold front that was moving eastward from the Great Lakes Region.  Aided by a strong jet streak aloft and a potent upper level shortwave trough, the cold front triggered a line of thunderstorms that produced damaging winds across western and central New York.||The line of thunderstorms reached eastern New York during the evening hours.  It continued to produce strong winds, which caused damage across the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley and eventually into the Saratoga area, Capital Region and Hudson Valley as well. Many trees and power lines were downed across Herkimer County.  In addition, a weak, brief tornado also occurred in the southeastern Adirondacks in North Creek.   The line of thunderstorms moved eastward and weakened as they reached the border with New England by the late evening hours.  As a result, the threat for severe weather ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down and on power lines on Snydertown Road in the town of Claverack due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite being behind a cold front, the region remained in a warm and humid air mass.  This allowed the atmosphere to become quite unstable.   ||As yet another cold front approached the region during the afternoon hours, clusters of thunderstorms developed across eastern upstate New York. For the third day in a row, thunderstorms produced reports of wind damage across the region, with a few reports of large hail as well, as the storms moved from west to east.  ||The threat for storms ended during the evening hours, as the cold front moved across the area and into New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on New Salem Road near the Punkintown Fairgrounds in the New Salem section of New Scotland due to thunderstorm winds.  A large pine tree was downed on a house and power lines were ripped from a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure was situated over the Great Lakes region on Sunday, July 27th.  An upper level shortwave, combined with an approaching warm front, allowed for strong to severe thunderstorms to break out during the afternoon hours on July 27th over the Capital Region.    Hail up to half-dollar size was reported, as well as damage to trees and power lines due to gusty thunderstorm winds.  ||Some additional thunderstorms occurred during the overnight period on Sunday night, as the storm's warm front lifted across the region.  In addition to many cloud to ground lightning strikes and a period of heavy rainfall, strong winds caused damage to trees across parts of Saratoga County. ||As the strong area of low pressure moved across upstate New York on Monday, July 28th, some additional thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon hours. Trees were downed due to thunderstorms winds across parts of Columbia and Dutchess Counties, as the storm's cold front moved through the area.  Cooler and less humid air worked into the area behind the front for Monday night, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down, with one of them on a house, on New Street in the village of Philmont due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although the day began with clouds, breaks in the clouds during the afternoon hours allowed the atmosphere to become unstable over the Catskills and mid-Hudson Valley of eastern New York.  As a surface trough and upper level shortwave approached from the Great Lakes, thunderstorms broke out over the region.   Although the thunderstorms weren't overly tall, strong winds aloft allowed the storms to rotate and produce damaging wind gusts, mainly to trees, across Ulster and Dutchess Counties during the evening hours.  With the loss of daytime heating, thunderstorm ended by the late-evening hours, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in the town of Milan due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place over the region on Thursday, July 3rd.  As a strong cold front approached from the west, a broken line of thunderstorms developed over central New York and moved eastward during the afternoon hours.  Aided by powerful winds aloft, the storms moved from west to east over the region between the late afternoon and early evening hours.  Many of the storms produced strong winds, causing damage to trees and power lines over much of the region.  The most concentrated damage occurred from one particularly severe thunderstorm, which caused intense damage between West Winfield in Herkimer County towards Minden in Montgomery County.  A National Weather Service Storm Survey determined a series of microbursts produced wind damage up to 100 MPH in these areas.  A few of the storms also produced large hail as well.||As the cold front moved through the area by later in the evening, the storms moved east of the region and the threat for severe weather ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down in Broadalbin during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and humid air mass was in place across the region on Wednesday, June 25th.  As a slow moving cold front approached from the west, bands of heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the boundary.  As these showers and thunderstorms repeatedly passed over the same locations, the heavy rainfall led to significant runoff, which caused flash flooding in some areas.  Many roads were closed due to the flooding and some homes were affected by water as well.  In addition, a few of the storms produced wind damage to trees and some damaging lightning strikes.  ||The threat for heavy rainfall and flooding ended late on the evening of June 25th, as the storm's cold front passed through the region from west to east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a transformer to explode at the intersection of State Route 22 and State Route 71 in the town of Hillsdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm southerly flow allowed for temperatures to reach well into the 80s to near 90 on Wednesday, July 2nd.  In addition, it was rather humid with dewpoints nearing 70 degrees.  With the warm and humid conditions in place, the atmosphere became quite unstable during the afternoon and evening hours.||Well ahead of a cold front, a surface trough was in place across upstate New York.  This caused thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon hours.  Many of these storms became severe, producing wind damage to trees and power lines.  A few storms also contained large hail.  In addition, the very humid air mass in place gave the storms the capability of producing very heavy rainfall in a short period of time.  This allowed for a few of the storms to produce flash flooding in urban areas, such as around the Capital Region.  ||In addition, these storms produced a significant amount of cloud to ground lightning.  Several highway employees were struck by lightning in Rotterdam during a thunderstorm, but luckily escaped serious injury.  ||Although most of the storm ended by the late afternoon hours, a few additional severe storms during during the evening across the western Adirondacks and western Mohawk Valley.  These storms ended by the late evening hours, as nocturnal influences helped wane the development of additional thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and wires were reported down along Columbia Turnpike (U.S. Routes 9 & 20) at the intersection with Spring Hurst Drive due to thunderstorm winds.

These showers and thunderstorms produced very heavy downpours in a short period of time, with up to two inches of rain falling over eastern Ulster County.  As a result of the heavy rainfall, flash flooding occurred in the Woodstock area, causing several roads to be closed.  The roads eventually reopened once showers ended and the water receded.

Hail up to half-dollar size was reported, as well as damage to trees and power lines due to gusty thunderstorm winds.  ||Some additional thunderstorms occurred during the overnight period on Sunday night, as the storm's warm front lifted across the region.  In addition to many cloud to ground lightning strikes and a period of heavy rainfall, strong winds caused damage to trees across parts of Saratoga County. ||As the strong area of low pressure moved across upstate New York on Monday, July 28th, some additional thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon hours. Trees were downed due to thunderstorms winds across parts of Columbia and Dutchess Counties, as the storm's cold front moved through the area.  Cooler and less humid air worked into the area behind the front for Monday night, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed at 1157 7th Avenue in Watervliet due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place over the region on Tuesday, July 8th.   The region was ahead of a strong cold front that was moving eastward from the Great Lakes Region.  Aided by a strong jet streak aloft and a potent upper level shortwave trough, the cold front triggered a line of thunderstorms that produced damaging winds across western and central New York.||The line of thunderstorms reached eastern New York during the evening hours.  It continued to produce strong winds, which caused damage across the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley and eventually into the Saratoga area, Capital Region and Hudson Valley as well. Many trees and power lines were downed across Herkimer County.  In addition, a weak, brief tornado also occurred in the southeastern Adirondacks in North Creek.   The line of thunderstorms moved eastward and weakened as they reached the border with New England by the late evening hours.  As a result, the threat for severe weather ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree and wires were reported down on Creek Road in Danube due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although the day began with clouds, breaks in the clouds during the afternoon hours allowed the atmosphere to become unstable over the Catskills and mid-Hudson Valley of eastern New York.  As a surface trough and upper level shortwave approached from the Great Lakes, thunderstorms broke out over the region.   Although the thunderstorms weren't overly tall, strong winds aloft allowed the storms to rotate and produce damaging wind gusts, mainly to trees, across Ulster and Dutchess Counties during the evening hours.  With the loss of daytime heating, thunderstorm ended by the late-evening hours, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down due to thunderstorm winds in Millbrook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass was in place on Wednesday, July 23rd, allowing the atmosphere to become quite unstable.  As a cold front approached from the west, clusters of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours.  Some of these thunderstorms became severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.  Thunderstorms moved east and weakened by the late evening hours, as the cold front crossed the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place over the region on Thursday, July 3rd.  As a strong cold front approached from the west, a broken line of thunderstorms developed over central New York and moved eastward during the afternoon hours.  Aided by powerful winds aloft, the storms moved from west to east over the region between the late afternoon and early evening hours.  Many of the storms produced strong winds, causing damage to trees and power lines over much of the region.  The most concentrated damage occurred from one particularly severe thunderstorm, which caused intense damage between West Winfield in Herkimer County towards Minden in Montgomery County.  A National Weather Service Storm Survey determined a series of microbursts produced wind damage up to 100 MPH in these areas.  A few of the storms also produced large hail as well.||As the cold front moved through the area by later in the evening, the storms moved east of the region and the threat for severe weather ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down near Newville in the town of Danube due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm southerly flow allowed for temperatures to reach well into the 80s to near 90 on Wednesday, July 2nd.  In addition, it was rather humid with dewpoints nearing 70 degrees.  With the warm and humid conditions in place, the atmosphere became quite unstable during the afternoon and evening hours.||Well ahead of a cold front, a surface trough was in place across upstate New York.  This caused thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon hours.  Many of these storms became severe, producing wind damage to trees and power lines.  A few storms also contained large hail.  In addition, the very humid air mass in place gave the storms the capability of producing very heavy rainfall in a short period of time.  This allowed for a few of the storms to produce flash flooding in urban areas, such as around the Capital Region.  ||In addition, these storms produced a significant amount of cloud to ground lightning.  Several highway employees were struck by lightning in Rotterdam during a thunderstorm, but luckily escaped serious injury.  ||Although most of the storm ended by the late afternoon hours, a few additional severe storms during during the evening across the western Adirondacks and western Mohawk Valley.  These storms ended by the late evening hours, as nocturnal influences helped wane the development of additional thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unoccupied barn at 251 Alcove Road in Coeymans Hollow was struck by lightning.  The barn burned down as a result of the lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass was in place on Tuesday, July 1st, which allowed for the atmosphere to become quite unstable.  A weak surface boundary moved east across upstate New York during the evening hours, which caused a broken line of showers and thunderstorms to develop.  One of these thunderstorms grew tall enough to become severe, producing wind damage across parts of southern Saratoga County.  The showers and thunderstorms moved eastward and weakened by the late evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reportedly downed on Middleline Road in Ballston Center due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a slow moving cold front, a southerly flow transported plenty of moisture into the region during the day on Tuesday, June 3rd.  With a moist and unstable air mass in place, plenty of slow moving showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the approaching boundary.  Some of these thunderstorms produced very heavy rain in a short period of time.  This led to some flash flooding, especially in urban and poor drainage areas.  In addition, a few of the thunderstorms also produced strong wind gusts, which caused damage to trees and power lines.  By the late evening, the threat for thunderstorms ended, as the frontal boundary pushed through and less humid air moved into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down in Hudson due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved from west to east across the region on Friday, May 16th. A southerly flow of air out of the Gulf of Mexico allowed for plenty of moisture to be available for this system to produce widespread heavy rain showers. As these slow moving showers repeatedly moved over the same area, flash flooding occurred in parts of the western Mohawk Valley in southern Herkimer County. This flooding resulted in the closing of a roadway in West Winfield.  As the cold front pushed eastward and rain tapered off later in the day, flooding ending as water receded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 51 was reportedly closed due to flash flooding from heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although the day began with clouds, breaks in the clouds during the afternoon hours allowed the atmosphere to become unstable over the Catskills and mid-Hudson Valley of eastern New York.  As a surface trough and upper level shortwave approached from the Great Lakes, thunderstorms broke out over the region.   Although the thunderstorms weren't overly tall, strong winds aloft allowed the storms to rotate and produce damaging wind gusts, mainly to trees, across Ulster and Dutchess Counties during the evening hours.  With the loss of daytime heating, thunderstorm ended by the late-evening hours, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on Crescent Avenue due to thunderstorm winds in Clintondale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place on the evening of Tuesday, June 17th.   A strong cold front moved across the region from west to east between late in the evening on Tuesday, June 17th and the early morning hours of Wednesday, June 18th.  Along and just ahead of this boundary, a line of severe thunderstorms in the form of a squall line raced from west to east across the area.  These storms produced significant wind damage across eastern New York.  The thunderstorms weakened somewhat as they reached the border with western New England as instability started to wane.  The threat for thunderstorms ended with the passage of the cold front by the late night hours, as drier and less humid air worked into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees were reported down in Jewett due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and humid air mass was in place across the region on Wednesday, June 25th.  As a slow moving cold front approached from the west, bands of heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the boundary.  As these showers and thunderstorms repeatedly passed over the same locations, the heavy rainfall led to significant runoff, which caused flash flooding in some areas.  Many roads were closed due to the flooding and some homes were affected by water as well.  In addition, a few of the storms produced wind damage to trees and some damaging lightning strikes.  ||The threat for heavy rainfall and flooding ended late on the evening of June 25th, as the storm's cold front passed through the region from west to east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down due to thunderstorm winds in White Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure was situated over the Great Lakes region on Sunday, July 27th.  An upper level shortwave, combined with an approaching warm front, allowed for strong to severe thunderstorms to break out during the afternoon hours on July 27th over the Capital Region.    Hail up to half-dollar size was reported, as well as damage to trees and power lines due to gusty thunderstorm winds.  ||Some additional thunderstorms occurred during the overnight period on Sunday night, as the storm's warm front lifted across the region.  In addition to many cloud to ground lightning strikes and a period of heavy rainfall, strong winds caused damage to trees across parts of Saratoga County. ||As the strong area of low pressure moved across upstate New York on Monday, July 28th, some additional thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon hours. Trees were downed due to thunderstorms winds across parts of Columbia and Dutchess Counties, as the storm's cold front moved through the area.  Cooler and less humid air worked into the area behind the front for Monday night, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported during a thunderstorm in Boght Corners.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and humid air mass was in place across the region on Wednesday, June 25th.  As a slow moving cold front approached from the west, bands of heavy rain showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the boundary.  As these showers and thunderstorms repeatedly passed over the same locations, the heavy rainfall led to significant runoff, which caused flash flooding in some areas.  Many roads were closed due to the flooding and some homes were affected by water as well.  In addition, a few of the storms produced wind damage to trees and some damaging lightning strikes.  ||The threat for heavy rainfall and flooding ended late on the evening of June 25th, as the storm's cold front passed through the region from west to east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms led to flash flooding in Saugerties.  U.S. Route 9W and  State Route 32 South (Partition Street) were closed, as they were impassable due to high water.
